scenario: Nimbus Insurance runs 4200 invoices per day through an approval process in Camunda 8.8. The team writes a process test for the timeout path where 5 days elapse on the approval user task and the process auto-approves. The test must advance the engine clock without touching real wall-clock time.
question: Which process test API call advances the engine clock by 5 days inside the test method?

options.text: Call processTestContext.increaseTime(Duration.ofDays(5)) to advance the engine clock before asserting the auto-approval completion path on the instance
Call processTestContext.advanceClock(Duration.ofDays(5)) to move the engine clock forward before asserting the auto-approval completion path on the instance
Call Thread.sleep(Duration.ofDays(5).toMillis()) to let the engine timer fire naturally before asserting the auto-approval completion path on the instance
Call client.newClockResetCommand().offset(Duration.ofDays(5)).send().join() to move the engine clock before asserting the auto-approval completion path

a.text: Correct. The documented timeout test uses processTestContext.increaseTime(Duration.ofDays(5)) to advance the engine clock so the approval timer fires inside the unit test.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented helper is named increaseTime, not advanceClock; the snippet shows processTestContext.increaseTime(Duration.ofDays(5)).

c.text: Incorrect. Thread.sleep would block the test for five days of real time; the docs use processTestContext.increaseTime to move the engine clock.

d.text: Incorrect. The source uses processTestContext.increaseTime(Duration.ofDays(5)); there is no clock-reset command shown for advancing time in the test.

explanation: In Camunda 8.8 process tests, processTestContext.increaseTime advances the engine clock so timer boundary events fire inside a unit test, as shown in the documented testApprovalTimeout example using Duration.ofDays(5).
